A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> and on the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> chromosome. A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #8B0000;">UNLINKED</strong> but may be on either the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> or <strong style="color: #D2691E;">DIFFERENT</strong> chromosomes. A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #5B2C6F;">NEITHER</strong> <strong>linked</strong> NOR <strong>unlinked</strong>. Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> and on the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> chromosome.	Correct

A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #5B2C6F;">NEITHER</strong> <strong>linked</strong> NOR <strong>unlinked</strong>.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> and on the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> chromosome.	Correct	The two genes are <strong style="color: #7B3F00;">BOTH</strong> <strong>linked</strong> AND <strong>unlinked</strong>. A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #8B0000;">UNLINKED</strong> but may be on either the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> or <strong style="color: #D2691E;">DIFFERENT</strong> chromosomes. A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #7B3F00;">BOTH</strong> <strong>linked</strong> AND <strong>unlinked</strong>.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> and on the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> chromosome.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #8B0000;">UNLINKED</strong> but may be on either the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> or <strong style="color: #D2691E;">DIFFERENT</strong> chromosomes. A budding yeast that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #954309;">Gene C</span> grows in dense, irregular clusters, with cells clumping together rather than spreading smoothly.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #007043;">Gene F</span></strong> is associated with the '<span style="color: #007043;"><i>fuzzy</i></span>' phenotype. A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> and on the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> chromosome.	Correct	The two genes are <strong style="color: #8B0000;">UNLINKED</strong> but may be on either the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> or <strong style="color: #D2691E;">DIFFERENT</strong> chromosomes.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> but are likely on <strong style="color: #D2691E;">DIFFERENT</strong> chromosomes.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #7B3F00;">BOTH</strong> <strong>linked</strong> AND <strong>unlinked</strong>.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #5B2C6F;">NEITHER</strong> <strong>linked</strong> NOR <strong>unlinked</strong>.	Incorrect

A budding yeast that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #8f3481;">Gene T</span> secretes a toxic compound that inhibits or kills other microbial colonies nearby.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; NPD &rarr; linked; PD &approx; NPD &rarr; unlinked</li></ul></ul><h6>Unordered Tetrad Two Gene Determine Linkage</h6><p>The yeast <i>Saccharomyces cerevisiae</i> has unordered tetrads. A cross is made to study the linkage relationships among two genes.</p> <p>Using the table above, determine the linkage between the two genes.</p>	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> but are likely on <strong style="color: #D2691E;">DIFFERENT</strong> chromosomes.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #7B3F00;">BOTH</strong> <strong>linked</strong> AND <strong>unlinked</strong>.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #006400;">LINKED</strong> and on the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> chromosome.	Correct	The two genes are <strong style="color: #8B0000;">UNLINKED</strong> but may be on either the <strong style="color: #1E40AF;">SAME</strong> or <strong style="color: #D2691E;">DIFFERENT</strong> chromosomes.	Incorrect	The two genes are <strong style="color: #5B2C6F;">NEITHER</strong> <strong>linked</strong> NOR <strong>unlinked</strong>.	Incorrect
